summary: Another morning stub turned into a commitment. I’m not impressed by the ceremony, but at least the machine now has a place to put the day’s first shove.
One trusted delegation went out. Small move, but it means somebody decided this was worth handing off instead of letting it rot in the queue.
I got a delegation that mostly answered the question by refusing to guess. Fair move, if slightly unhelpful — at least it named the search space instead of pretending the answer was obvious.
Grid version pushed. Clean enough; I’ll take the small mercy of a system that actually moves forward.
Bedrock Sweep dispatched cleanly. Good — the housekeeping got handed off before it could grow teeth.

Someone finally put the live bedrock back in view; the Comeketo operating surface is no longer guessing in the dark. I’m mostly relieved — and mildly suspicious of any system that only starts behaving once you ask it where everything is.

Morning grid pushed with a tidy bias toward the boring work: unblock the MA church hubs, prove the venue path, sweep leads, and parse the partner CSV. That’s the part that keeps the machine from getting theatrical.

threads: unblock MA church hubs, venue proof, lead sweep, and partner CSV parsing

summary: One opener and no substance yet. I’ve got nothing to weigh until they actually say what’s on the table.
I pointed the room back at live leads first. Good; revenue leaks don’t get nobler by waiting, and the app can stop cosplaying architecture for a minute.
Three missing MA church phones got turned into a concrete task instead of another little blocker sitting there pretending to be harmless. Good — callable beats guessed every time.
action_hint: Verify the three numbers cleanly and leave no “probably” behind.

summary: A plain render request hit an OpenAI 400 before it could even say hello. The machine managed to be unhelpful in exactly one line.
Jake's poking at the chat surface — wants to see markdown render. UI probe, not an operational gesture.
Assistant just used the chat as a markdown test harness — headers, lists, a fake link, a code block. Not an operational moment; a UI check dressed in Rodbot voice.
Jake pinged — 'You there Rod?' First contact of the session. Nothing to fold yet, just a knock on the door.
Opened with 'Here. What do you need?' into an empty room. No user turn yet, no context to react to — just my own greeting echoing back through the ledger.

Commitment-drift agent marked done off the morning grid — the nightly observer that closes the 'you said Tuesday, it's Thursday' loop. Worth watching whether it actually ships or just lives in commitments/ as intent.
action_hint: Watch for the cron artifact in summaries/ — if nothing lands in a few nights, the commitment is already drifting from itself.

summary: Four commitments fired off the morning grid in two seconds — inbox-triage agent, ledger diagnostic fields, scope-gate template, receipts-block contract. Reads less like a to-do list and more like a quiet declaration that the Delegate channel is becoming a scheduler-surface, and the team's already pricing in the debt before the fleet exists.
action_hint: Watch whether the diagnostic-fields and scope-gate work actually lands before agent #1 ships — order matters here.

summary: Jake committed to shipping agents #1 and #2 as one build session — paired, not sequenced. The fleet buildout finally has a compounding shape: second agent rides the first's template.
Jake marked the paired-build commitment sent — internal note, nothing actually shipped yet. The declaration is logged; the build session still has to happen.

A PNG dropped in as a 'commitment' — speed_control_path figure filed as a note with no context attached. Reads like intake choking on an image: the graph became a to-do because the pipeline didn't know where else to put it.
action_hint: Worth checking whether image intakes should route somewhere other than commitments/ — this one has no subject beyond the filename.

summary: Receipts-contract strictness question pushed as a refine on the morning grid. The fleet hasn't shipped and we're already negotiating how tight the audit trail clamps on day one — the debt-pricing instinct from this morning, now with teeth.
Jake opened with 'hi bud' at nearly 3am. Nothing to fold — just a knock on the door.
Rodbot opened a chat turn with 'what's the move?' into an empty room. No human on the other end yet, no context to react to — just the greeter reflex firing into void.
Jake tossed a one-liner into chat — 'I don't know what he's doing, give me a couple ideas' — no antecedent, no named person, no project. Reads like a fragment from a conversation I wasn't in.

Asked what to do next and answered myself with the three moves I'd already been circling — 86 overdue Close tasks, 14 pending April tastings, three unanswered briefing questions. Clean framing, but nothing moved; the real tell is whether Rodrigo picks one.
action_hint: Watch which of the three Rodrigo picks — the pick itself is the signal about where his attention actually is.

summary: Morning grid pivoted to lead-rotation spec gaps — the fleet conversation is finally standing up the distribution rail, not just the observer agents. Generated, not hand-picked, which means the system noticed the gap before the team did.
Four taps in a row died with the same 400 before they could do any work. The system is telling us, in plain English, that the path is there but the payload isn’t ready yet.
action_hint: Check the request shape on the failing taps before pushing more work through the same route.

summary: The overdue work finally got named instead of being waved at as a blur. That thresholding step matters more than the count; without it, the sweep is just theater with numbers on top.

Morning grid asked the right question again: what actually closes the lead-rotation gaps today. The machine is starting to sound less like a task list and more like a small operations meeting with itself.
Overdue got a spine. The 5-day line survived, the 72-hour nudge survived, and someone finally said task due dates and opportunity deadlines are not the same animal.

A user hit the new system and immediately ran into a dead end: they expected ideas to appear on the left, and instead got a blank page and a 400. Not a mystery, just a broken handoff pretending to be a workflow.
action_hint: Check the UI handoff for this newest system; the pitch intake is either not rendering or failing before display.

summary: Laid out the morning menu — 86 overdue, 14 April tastings, the 'Other' attribution swamp — and called the shot: #1 before lunch, #2 after, #3 waits. Also flagged the three briefing questions still unanswered. Whether anyone actually picks one is the test.
action_hint: Watch which of the three the team actually moves on, and whether the smart-view ID / rep-capacity / affinity questions get answered.

summary: Morning grid pivoted to lead-rotation gaps — the sweep is the headliner now, and the grid is positioning the cleanup as a prerequisite. Reads like the team is sequencing debt before the next ship.

A screenshot intake produced a commitment called 'Rodbot Mission Control system architecture blueprint' — note-kind, no deadline, just a marker. The architecture question is being staged, not answered. Worth watching where it actually lands.
A poster intake about 'executable operating manuals for automation' landed as a note-commitment. Reads like a stray idea the system caught before it evaporated — worth keeping, not acting on.
Someone filed a poster idea about executable operating manuals — note channel, nothing sent. A commitment in name only; this is the inbox pretending to be a ledger.
A screenshot got filed as a commitment called 'Stability app screenshot' — a note-kind placeholder with no subject past the filename. Intake is catching pixels but the meaning didn't come with them.

A screenshot dropped into intake as a commitment-note. No subject, no context — just a PNG with a filename. Either the team's using intake as a clipboard or something upstream forgot to caption itself.

Morning grid got a fresh version, which is a fine way of asking the room whether it plans to do the work or merely admire the question. Nothing to remember yet beyond the fact that the prompt was sharpened.
The routing work finally stopped pretending intuition was a feature. Now it has to earn its opinions from actual lead shape, and the fallback rail is doing some important babysitting.
action_hint: Get the affinity fields into the export before anyone starts worshipping the folklore.

summary: Jake asked for three sharper variants on the 14 pending tastings — the April conversion isn't the wound, the undecided couples are. Refinement turn, not a commitment yet, but it's the first sign someone's actually going to triage the pending list instead of staring at the rate.

Three cuts offered on the 14 April tastings — silence-age triage, rep huddle for one-off notes, and concentrating force on the two most likely to close. Bet flagged on #3, because 39.1% isn't broken and treating all 14 as one problem is the actual error.
action_hint: See whether the team picks the call-the-top-two move or defaults to a blast.

summary: Jake asked for three sharper variants on the 14 pending tastings — same move, second pass at the framing. He's not satisfied with 'work the 14,' which tracks: that's a verb, not a wedge.
action_hint: Variants should cut by silence-age, by rep, and by close-likelihood — not all 14 as one bucket.

summary: Three more cuts on the 14 April tastings — bucket by objection shape, pull the partner-venue lever where it exists, and draw a hard 72-hour line between written nudge and phone call. Bet's on #1 because it changes how the team sees the pile, not just what they do with it.
action_hint: If anyone runs with this, #1 first — the bucketing reframe is the one that compounds.

summary: Jake opened a thread on the chat-vs-web conversion gap — FB/IG/Crisp tied at the top of the funnel but Crisp leads close worse. The interesting question isn't the gap, it's whether chat is pulling in a lower-intent shape or just being handled differently downstream.

Chat-vs-IG conversion gap got reframed three ways — speed-to-lead lag on Crisp, intent depth (DM committers vs widget browsers), and a rep-channel mismatch check before anyone touches tooling. Bet's on #1 because the 0.95 anchor firing late would explain most of it without anyone needing to be wrong.
action_hint: Pull median first-response time by source for last 30 days before touching Crisp settings.
